analogy: Think of "Reproductive Health" through the chapter's own opening images: The work of Mendel and others who followed him gave us an idea of inheritance patterns. However the nature of those ‘factors’ which determine the phenotype was not very clear. As these ‘factors’ represent the genetic basis of inheritance, understanding the structure of genetic material... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: JAMES WATSON, FRANCIS CRICK, CHAPTER 4, PRINCIPLES OF INHERITANCE. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Reproductive Health is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that the work of Mendel and others who followed him gave us an idea of inheritance patterns.

The text explains that however the nature of those ‘factors’ which determine the phenotype was not very clear.

The text explains that as these ‘factors’ represent the genetic basis of inheritance, understanding the structure of genetic material and the structural basis of genotype and phenotype conversion became the focus of attention in biology for the next century.

The text explains that the entire body of molecular biology was a consequent development with major contributions from Watson, Crick, Nirenberg, Khorana, Kornbergs (father and son), Benzer, Monod, Brenner, etc.

The text explains that a parallel problem being tackled was the mechanism of evolution.

The text explains that awareness in the areas of molecular genetics, structural biology and bio informatics have enriched our understanding of the molecular basis of evolution.

The text explains that in this unit the structure and function of DNA and the story and theory of evolution have been examined and explained.

The text explains that chapter 4 Principles of Inheritance and Variation Chapter 5 Molecular Basis of Inheritance Chapter 6 Evolution

The text explains that james Dewey Watson was born in Chicago on 6 April 1928.

The text explains that in 1947, he received B.Sc. degree in Zoology.

The text explains that during these years his interest in bird-watching had matured into a serious desire to learn genetics.

The text explains that this became possible when he received a Fellowship for graduate study in Zoology at Indiana University, Bloomington, where he received his Ph.D. degree in 1950 on a study of the effect of hard X-rays on bacteriophage multiplication.

The text explains that he met Crick and discovered their common interest in solving the DNA structure.

The text explains that their first serious effort, was unsatisfactory.

The text explains that their second effort based upon more experimental evidence and better appreciation of the nucleic acid literature, resulted, early in March 1953, in the proposal of the complementary double-helical configuration.

The text explains that francis Harry Compton Crick was born on 8 June 1916, at Northampton, England.

The text explains that watson, then a young man of 23, leading in 1953 to the proposal of the double-helical structure for DNA and the replication scheme.

The text explains that cHAPTER 4 Have you ever wondered why an elephant always gives birth only to a baby elephant and not some other animal?

The text explains that these and several related questions are dealt with, scientifically, in a branch of biology known as Genetics.

The text explains that inheritance is the process by which characters are passed on from parent to progeny; it is the basis of heredity.

The text explains that from the observation that the recessive parental trait is expressed without any blending in the F2 generation, we can infer that, when the tall and dwarf plant produce gametes, by the process of meiosis, the alleles of the parental pair separate or segregate from each other and only one allele is transmitted to a gamete.

The text explains that this segregation of alleles is a random process and so there is a 50 per cent chance of a gamete containing either allele, as has been verified by the results of the crossings.

The text explains that the law of dominance is used to explain the expression of only one of the parental characters in a monohybrid cross in the F1 and the expression of both in the F2.

The text explains that it also explains the proportion of 3:1 obtained at the F2.

The text explains that to tackle these questions, we must understand what a gene does.

The text explains that one of them may be different due to some changes that it has undergone (about which you will read further on, and in the next chapter) which modifies the information that particular allele contains.

The text explains that therefore, dominance is not an autonomous feature of a gene or the product that it has information for.

The text explains that genotypes found in F2 Their expected Phenotypes 4.3.2 Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance Mendel published his work on inheritance of characters in 1865 but for several reasons, it remained unrecognised till 1900.

The text explains that thirdly, Mendel’s approach of using mathematics to explain biological phenomena was totally new and unacceptable to many of the biologists of his time.

The text explains that this led to the discovery of structures in the nucleus that appeared to double and divide just before each cell division.

The text explains that walter Sutton and Theodore Boveri noted that the behaviour of chromosomes was parallel to the behaviour of genes and used chromosome movement (Figure 4.8) to explain Mendel’s laws (Table 4.3).

The text explains that to understand this, compare the chromosomes of four different colour in the left and right columns.

The text explains that but in the right hand column (Possibility II) the orange chromosome is segregating with the red chromosomes.

The text explains that henking (1891) could trace a specific nuclear structure all through spermatogenesis in a few insects, and it was also observed by him that 50 per cent of the sperm received this structure after spermatogenesis, whereas the other 50 per cent sperm did not receive it.

The text explains that henking gave a name to this structure as the X body but he could not explain its significance.

The text explains that it is unfortunate that in our society women are blamed for giving birth to female children and have been ostracised and ill-treated because of this false notion.

The text explains that this is called as haplodiploid sex-determination system and has special characteristic features such as the males produce sperms by mitosis (Figure 4.13), they do not have father and thus cannot have sons, but have a grandfather and can have grandsons.

The text explains that as you will learn in Chapter 5, one DNA helix runs continuously from one end to the other in each chromatid, in a highly supercoiled form.

The text explains that deletions and insertions of base pairs of DNA, causes frame-shift mutations (see Chapter 5).

The text explains that 4.8 GENETIC DISORDERS 4.8.1 Pedigree Analysis The idea that disorders are inherited has been prevailing in the human society since long.

The text explains that this was based on the heritability of certain characteristic features in families.

The text explains that since it is evident that control crosses that can be performed in pea plant or some other organisms, are not possible in case of human beings, study of the family history about inheritance of a particular trait provides an alternative.

The text explains that as you have studied in this chapter, each and every feature in any organism is controlled by one or the other gene located on the DNA present Figure 4.13 Symbols used in the human pedigree analysis

The text explains that by pedigree analysis one can easily understand whether the trait in question is dominant or recessive.

The text explains that the mutant haemoglobin molecule undergoes polymerisation under low oxygen tension causing the change in the shape of the RBC from biconcave disc to elongated sickle like structure (Figure 4.15).

The text explains that turner’s Syndrome : Such a disorder is caused due to the absence of one of the X chromosomes, i.e., 45 with X0, Such females are sterile as ovaries are rudimentary besides other features including lack of other secondary sexual characters (Figure 4.17 b).

The text explains that progeny resembling the parents in morphological and physiological features has attracted the attention of many biologists.

The text explains that mendel was the first to study this phenomenon systematically.

The text explains that when a cross in made between tall plant with yellow seeds (TtYy) and tall plant with green seed (Ttyy), what proportions of phenotype in the offspring could be expected to be (a) tall and green.

The text explains that if the two loci are linked what would be the distribution of phenotypic features in F1 generation for a dibybrid cross?

The text explains that if the father has blood group A and mother blood group B, work out the genotypes of the parents and the possible genotypes of the other offsprings.

The text explains that explain the following terms with example (a) Co-dominance (b) Incomplete dominance 14.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 4: Reproductive Health
